methods.description: Analysis of tephra samples under a binocular microscope to evaluate the relative abundance of glass-shards, organic material, and detrital material. Glass-shard morphology, angularity, and colour were described. Dominant minerals and the state of their glass-coatings were noted. These data, combined with chemical analyses, are used to provide information on depositional nature and distinguish between primary or reworked events.

steps.description: The samples were analysed under a binocular microscope to evaluate the relative abundance of: glass-shards organic material detrital material Glass-shard morphology, angularity (Fig. 2), and colour were also described.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform microscopic analysis of tephra samples to evaluate component abundance, glass-shard morphology, angularity, and colour.
outputs: Relative abundance data (glass-shards, organic material, detrital material)

steps.description: The dominant minerals present were additionally noted, including whether minerals predominantly had preserved or degraded glass-coatings.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify dominant minerals and assess the condition of their glass-coatings.

steps.description: These data (in addition to chemical analyses; Section 2.3.2) were used to provide detailed further information on the depositional nature and help distinguish between primary or reworked events (e.g., McLean et al., 2018;Supplementary File 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret visual data (and chemical data) to determine depositional nature and distinguish primary/reworked events.

methods.method_name: Classification of Tephra Layers (Primary vs Reworked)
methods.description: A point-based criteria system used to distinguish between primary and reworked tephra layers. Points are assigned based on indicators of reworking, and deposits with three or more points are considered reworked, subject to an overall assessment considering lithology, contact, and previous reports.

steps.description: The criteria that were used are as follows, and deposits received one point for each of these features identified: minerals in the sample are rounded or sub-rounded; minerals have no glass coating, or glass-coating is degraded; glass-shard abundance is low ( 60%); high abundance of organic material (!5%); high abundance of detrital material (!10%); chemistry of glass-shards is the same as those in the tephra layer/s below.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify and record the presence of specific reworking indicators in the sample.
outputs: Presence/absence of reworking indicators (rounded/sub-rounded minerals, degraded/absent glass coating, low glass-shard abundance, high organic material, high detrital material, matching chemistry to layers below)

steps.description: deposits received one point for each of these features identified
steps.searchable_summary: Assign one point for each identified reworking characteristic.
outputs: Total reworking points for the sample

steps.description: deposits which had three or more points relating to reworking characteristics were considered as reworked.
steps.searchable_summary: Determine if the total reworking points meet or exceed the threshold (3 points) for preliminary classification as reworked.

steps.description: Before concluding the classification, these deposits were also considered as a whole afterwards (e.g., appearance in lithology, including contact, and previous reports on layer).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform an overall assessment considering lithology, contact, and previous reports to finalize the classification.

methods.description: Procedures for preparing tephra samples from terrestrial and pelagic environments for electron microprobe analysis, including crushing, sieving, heavy liquid separation, mounting, sectioning, polishing, and carbon coating.

steps.description: Tephra samples from terrestrial environments were gently crushed with a pestle and mortar before being wet-sieved using 80 mm nylon mesh and demineralised water.
steps.searchable_summary: Crush terrestrial tephra samples and wet-sieve using 80 mm mesh.

steps.description: Visible tephra samples from pelagic sediments were wet-sieved using 25 mm nylon mesh and demineralised water;
steps.searchable_summary: Wet-sieve pelagic tephra samples using 25 mm mesh.

steps.description: organics and minerals were removed from the tephra samples, and then glass-shards were isolated using heavy liquid density separation techniques as per Blockley et al. (2005) to extract components with densities between 2.0 g cm 3 and 2.5 g cm 3 .
steps.searchable_summary: Remove organics and minerals, then isolate glass-shards using heavy liquid density separation (2.0-2.5 g/cm³).

steps.description: Glass-shards were subsequently mounted in Epoxy resin stubs,
steps.searchable_summary: Mount isolated glass-shards in Epoxy resin stubs.

steps.description: which were sectioned and polished to expose a flat surface,
steps.searchable_summary: Section and polish the resin stubs to expose a flat surface of the glass-shards.
outputs: Polished resin stubs with exposed glass-shard surfaces

steps.description: before being carbon coated for chemical analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: Apply carbon coating to the polished stubs.

methods.method_name: Electron Microprobe (EMP) Analysis of Glass-Shard Composition
methods.description: Analysis of the major element composition of prepared glass-shards using a wavelength-dispersive JEOL JXA-8600 electron microprobe. The procedure includes setting instrument parameters, analyzing elements with specific counting times, calibrating with primary standards, verifying with secondary standards, filtering data based on total weight percentage, normalizing data, and visualizing results.

steps.description: The major element composition of glass-shards was analysed at the Research Laboratory for Archaeology and the History of Art, University of Oxford, using a wavelength-dispersive JEOL JXA-8600 electron microprobe (EMP) equipped with four spectrometers, and an accelerating voltage of 15 kV.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform major element analysis of glass-shards using a JEOL JXA-8600 EMP with 15 kV accelerating voltage.

steps.description: Shards were predominantly analysed with a low beam current of 6 nA and a 10 mm beam diameter;
steps.searchable_summary: Set EMP beam current to 6 nA and beam diameter to 10 mm for standard analysis.
outputs: EMP configured with 6 nA current, 10 mm diameter

steps.description: very small shards were analysed with a 5 mm beam, and a 4 nA current.
steps.searchable_summary: Set EMP beam current to 4 nA and beam diameter to 5 mm for analysis of very small shards.
outputs: EMP configured with 4 nA current, 5 mm diameter

steps.description: Thirty shards were analysed for each sample where possible.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze a target of thirty glass-shards per sample.
outputs: Major element data for up to 30 shards per sample

steps.description: Sodium (Na) was analysed first to minimise element loss due to migration.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze Sodium (Na) first in the elemental analysis sequence.

steps.description: Elemental peaks were collected for a total of 60 s (P), 50 s (Cl), 40 s (Mn), 30 s (Si, Ca, K, Al, Ti, Fe, Mg), or 12 s (Na)
steps.searchable_summary: Set EMP peak counting times for specific elements.
outputs: EMP configured with specific peak counting times

steps.description: and for half of the total peak time on each of the background positions on either side of the peak.
steps.searchable_summary: Set EMP background counting times (half of peak time) and positions.

steps.description: Prior to analysis, the EMP was calibrated for each element using primary mineral standards.
steps.searchable_summary: Calibrate the EMP for each element using primary mineral standards.

steps.description: To verify the calibration, Max Planck Institute reference glasses (MPI-DING) were used as secondary standards; StHs6-80/G, ATHO-G, and GOR132-G (Jochum et al., 2006) were analysed at the beginning, during and at the end of all sample runs.
steps.searchable_summary: Analyze secondary reference standards (MPI-DING glasses) at the beginning, during, and end of sample runs to verify calibration.
outputs: Quality control data from secondary standard analyses

steps.description: The averaged values of these lie within two standard deviations of the preferred values (see Jochum et al., 2006).
steps.searchable_summary: Check if the averaged values of secondary standards are within two standard deviations of preferred values.
inputs: Quality control data from secondary standard analyses

steps.description: Micro-phenocrysts, vesicles and resin were avoided during analyses.
steps.searchable_summary: Select analysis spots on glass-shards, avoiding micro-phenocrysts, vesicles, and resin.

steps.description: Preferable total values for unknown samples are !95e100.5 wt% (e.g., Shane, 2000;Lowe, 2011;Fontijn et al., 2016). To maximise the retention of analyses where devitrification has started to occur, lower total values of >94e100.5 wt% are considered acceptable (Shane, 2000;Fontijn et al., 2016).
steps.searchable_summary: Filter EMP analyses based on total oxide weight percentage, accepting >94 wt% and preferably !95-100.5 wt%.
inputs: Raw major element composition data (wt% totals)
outputs: Filtered dataset retaining analyses within acceptable total wt% range

steps.description: To account for variable secondary hydration, data were normalised to 100 wt%.
steps.searchable_summary: Normalize major element data to 100 wt% (anhydrous basis).

steps.description: Individual glass-shard chemical analyses are shown as biplots, particularly using SiO 2 /K 2 O, as potassium is the most distinguishing element of SVZ and AVZ deposits due to along-arc variations in the potassium composition of the melt (Kilian et al., 2003;Stern, 2008;Wastegård et al., 2013).
steps.searchable_summary: Generate biplots (scatter plots) of normalized major element data, specifically SiO2 vs K2O.
